topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white against the drab beige of the university hallway, felt like a physical assault. Liam stared at the printed document, his knuckles white where he gripped the edge of the desk. Every word—*permanent closure, effective next semester*—reverberated in the sudden, heavy silence of his cramped dorm room. He slammed the paper onto the floor, the sound sharp and brittle. "Are you serious?" he muttered, the disbelief thick in his throat. He paced the small space three times, his boots thudding unevenly on the cheap carpet. He felt a hot, prickly sensation rising up his neck, a tight knot constricting his chest. He wanted to shout, to tear the notice to shreds, but his voice caught, coming out as a strangled, low growl. This wasn't just disappointing; it felt like a deliberate, cruel joke played on everyone who had poured their futures into these crumbling brick walls.

[story 2]

"Unbelievable." Sarah whispered, the sound barely audible over the distant hum of the campus HVAC system. She stood by the window, watching the autumn leaves swirl in a frantic dance, mirroring the turmoil inside her. She reread the final paragraph of the announcement, her eyes scanning the text as if searching for a loophole, a hidden clause that would invalidate the entire declaration. A tremor ran through her hands, causing her to clutch the worn strap of her backpack until her fingers ached. She turned sharply, addressing the empty room. "They just... gave up? After everything?" Her jaw was clenched so tightly she could feel the strain in her molars. She began pacing the perimeter of her room, her movements jerky and agitated, kicking at a loose floorboard with unnecessary force. The injustice of it burned behind her eyes.

[story 3]

Mark leaned against the cold cinderblock wall of the administrative building, his breath coming in short, shallow gasps. He hadn't processed the news; he was simply reacting to the sheer absurdity of it. He ran a hand roughly through his already messy hair, pulling at the strands as if trying to physically pull the situation apart. "This is ridiculous," he said aloud, the words laced with a sharp, grating edge. He started walking again, a restless energy propelling him down the corridor, his pace quickening until he was nearly jogging. His shoulders were hunched, rigid with a simmering frustration that made his jaw lock tight. He stopped abruptly, spinning around to glare at the imposing façade of the main hall. He felt a profound sense of being cheated, a burning resentment that settled deep in his stomach, demanding an outlet.
